questions.text: The strength of an acid depends on?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.

Stability of the anion formed: After the acid donates its proton, the resulting anion (conjugate base) is formed. A stable anion is better able to handle the negative charge that results from gaining an electron. An unstable or highly reactive anion will make the acid weaker.
Stability is not created by cation formation.

options.text: Depends on both ease of removal of proton and stability of the anion formed
options.explanation: The strength of an acid depends on both the ease of removal of a proton and the stability of the anion formed after the proton donation.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: Depends on both ease of removal of a proton and stability of the anion formed.Explanation:The strength of an acid is determined by its ability to donate a proton (H+) in a chemical reaction. This is often represented by the equation:Acid (AH) ⇌ H+ + Anion (A-)In this equation, the acid (AH) donates a proton (H+) to form the conjugate base or anion (A-). The strength of an acid is influenced by two main factors:Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.Stability of the anion formed: After the acid donates its proton, the resulting anion (conjugate base) is formed. A stable anion is better able to handle the negative charge that results from gaining an electron. An unstable or highly reactive anion will make the acid weaker.Together, these factors play a crucial role in determining the strength of an acid. Acids with both a high tendency to release protons (ease of removal of a proton) and produce stable anions are considered strong acids. On the other hand, weak acids have a lower tendency to donate protons and/or form less stable anions.In summary, the strength of an acid depends on both the ease of removal of a proton and the stability of the anion formed after the proton donation.

Bromine solution is naturally orange and when consumed in oxidation, it becomes colourless.2, 4 DNPH is used to detect ketones and aldehydes. questions.text: The general formula for an aldehyde is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is wrong as R-OH is the general formula of alcohol.
Option B is wrong R-COOH is the general formula of carboxylic acid.
Option C is wrong as R-CO-R is the general formula of ketone.

The general formula for the aldehyde group is R-CHO or R-COH. The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde with the formula HCOH.Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3).An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. All other aldehydes have one hydrogen bonded to the carbonyl group, like the simple molecule acetaldehyde, which has one hydrogen and one methyl group (HCOCH3).

questions.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3).An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. All other aldehydes have one hydrogen bonded to the carbonyl group, like the simple molecule acetaldehyde, which has one hydrogen and one methyl group (HCOCH3).The carbonyl carbon in both aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic, meaning that it has a dipole due to the electronegativity of the attached oxygen atom. This makes the carbonyl carbon an ideal target for nucleophiles in a nucleophilic addition reaction. During this reaction, the nucleophile, or electron donor, attacks the carbonyl to form the tetrahedral intermediate. The negatively charged oxygen accepts a hydrogen ion to form a hydroxyl group.Typically, nucleophiles possess a negative charge or lone pair on a heteroatom, which can take several forms (OH-, RO-, CN-, R3C-, RNH2, ROH). For primary amines (RNH2), the reaction does not stop at the formation of the tetrahedral intermediate with a hydroxyl group. Rather, an elimination reaction occurs that produces a double-bonded carbon and nitrogen functional group known as an imine. Understanding the reactions that aldehydes and ketones can undergo provides a way to differentiate between these similar organic compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry

Elimination reaction is a chemical reaction that removes one or more atoms or groups of atoms from a molecule. Benzene does not undergo elimination reactions.

When benzene is heated in air with V2O5 at 450°C, it undergoes oxidation to form maleic anhydride. The reaction is an example of a free radical oxidation reaction. questions.text: What is the electrophile in the acylation of benzene

options.explanation: AlCl3 is a Lewis acid, but it is not the electrophile in the acylation of benzene.

questions.explanation: Influenza is caused specifically by the influenza virus because it belongs to the Orthomyxoviridae family, which has unique properties — it is an enveloped, negative-sense RNA virus with a segmented genome that determines the typical flu symptoms (fever, cough, sore throat, body aches). Other viruses you mentioned — herpesviruses (DNA viruses), variola virus causing smallpox (Poxviridae, DNA virus), and adenoviruses (DNA viruses) — belong to completely different viral families with distinct structures, replication strategies, and disease profiles. They cause illnesses like cold sores, chickenpox/shingles (herpes family), smallpox (variola), or respiratory and eye infections (adenovirus), but not influenza. In short, disease causation is tied to the specific virus family and its biology, so only influenza viruses can cause flu.
